interview_metadata.interviewee_title: St. Augustine Civil Rights Activist; Retired New York City Police Lieutenant
interview_metadata.interview_location: Bayfront Hilton Hotel, St. Augustine, Florida
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

main_summary.summary:
Purcell Maurice Conway was interviewed in St. Augustine, Florida on September 13, 2011 by Joseph Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with videographer John Bishop, for the Civil Rights History Project jointly sponsored by the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture. Born in 1948, Conway grew up in Lincolnville, the historically Black neighborhood of St. Augustine, raised after age five by his great-great-grandmother, Mrs. Robinson, who was of Seminole Indian and Spanish descent. He recalls the Galimore Center, then the only Black recreational park in the city, as a sanctuary in an otherwise segregated environment. At age ten he sold newspapers for the St. Augustine Record, including in front of the Castillo de San Marcos, where his early success was disrupted when white paper boys were stationed beside him and customers redirected their purchases.

Conway describes growing into civil rights activism in his teens, around age fourteen or fifteen, after listening to Florida Memorial College students discuss demonstrations and nonviolent direct action. While he respected Dr. (Martin Luther) King, he was drawn at the time more toward the rhetoric of Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X, but joined the nonviolent demonstrations alongside other St. Augustine youth. He recounts a pivotal incident in which a white family friend served him a sandwich next to her dog's bowl while he cut her lawn, prompting his mother Julia Conway to become an activist herself. Conway participated in many marches; his mother, father, and three brothers were arrested on one occasion. He recounts a wade-in demonstration at St. Augustine Beach, organized in the wake of the Civil Rights Act, in which a white organizer he identifies as 'Al Lengo' was beaten unconscious by a mob carrying bats, pipes, and chains. A subsequent wade-in saw Florida state troopers, whom Conway believed had been ordered down by Governor Farris Bryant, drive Klansmen and their sympathizers out of the surf in a violent melee. He also describes being cornered by two policemen and a German Shepherd in the white area near the St. Augustine Record after intervening to protect a young white tutor and her Black students.

Conway recalls Hosea Williams organizing the second wade-in, and being a 'fly on the wall' as Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling, Dr. (Martin Luther) King, (Ralph) Abernathy, and Hosea Williams convened in St. Augustine. White college student tutors from Princeton, Massachusetts, and other Northern locations -- including Gail Blattenberg, with whom he later reconnected -- changed his view of white people; he stayed two weeks with her family in Princeton, New Jersey after his mother relented to a picketing campaign the students staged in front of his home. After enrolling in a brick masonry program at a Daytona Beach junior college and watching two white students receive their certificates after only a few months while he and his Black classmates were denied theirs, Conway moved to New York with his wife (whom he met at Bethune-Cookman). He worked five years in the Post Office, then joined the New York City Police Department, where he led successful discrimination lawsuits over the sergeant's and lieutenant's promotional exams that produced over 300 minority sergeants and became national legal precedents. Conway closes by paying tribute to his mother Julia Conway, his father George Conway (whose white butcher coworker threatened to quit if his employer fired George for marching), and the ACCORD organization led by Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and Mrs. Barbara Vickers, which has worked to memorialize the St. Augustine foot soldiers.
key_themes: Nonviolent Direct Action and the St. Augustine Movement
Racial Violence and Klan Confrontation at the Beach
Legal Activism Inside the New York City Police Department
main_summary.historical_significance: Conway's testimony adds a youth-eyewitness layer to the St. Augustine campaign of 1963-64, a movement central to the political pressure that forced passage of the Civil Rights Act of 1964. His firsthand accounts of two St. Augustine Beach wade-ins -- including state troopers ordered down by the governor physically driving Klansmen out of the surf -- and his vantage point on Dr. Robert B. Hayling, Hosea Williams, Dr. Martin Luther King, and Ralph Abernathy organizing in Lincolnville offer corroboration of the canonical narrative from a foot-soldier perspective. His later career as a NYPD lieutenant who led multi-plaintiff promotional-exam discrimination lawsuits extends the historical arc of the movement into Northern institutional civil rights litigation, documenting how movement experience prepared a generation to challenge structural racism in workplaces well beyond the South.

errors: The summary names 'Al Lengo' as the white organizer at the first wade-in based on Conway's transcript phrasing ('I think his name was Al Lengo'). Conway himself flags uncertainty ('I think his name'); the name is rendered ambiguously in the transcript and is not independently corroborated within the transcript or against civil_rights_facts.json. The summary preserves Conway's hedge by quoting the name, but readers may treat this as a confirmed identification.

The summary states the wade-ins occurred 'in the wake of the Civil Rights Act,' but the transcript references the Civil Rights Act in passing and Conway's chronology is impressionistic; the most violent St. Augustine wade-ins historically occurred in June 1964, just before the Act's July 2, 1964 passage. The transcript does not pin down whether Conway's specific recounted wade-ins were pre- or post-passage, so the summary's framing is mildly imprecise relative to what the transcript establishes.
improvements: Replace 'Al Lengo' with 'a white organizer Conway tentatively recalls as Al Lengo' to mirror Conway's hedge.
Soften 'in the wake of the Civil Rights Act' to 'during the St. Augustine wade-in campaign' since the transcript's chronological cues are loose.
Note explicitly that Conway's reference to white tutors picketing his home to convince his mother is a striking moment worth surfacing earlier in the summary.

chapters.title: Interview Introduction and Project Framing
chapters.summary: Joseph Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, joined by videographer John Bishop, opens the interview at the Bayfront Hilton Hotel in St. Augustine, Florida on September 13, 2011. He notes the interview is one of a set focused on St. Augustine civil rights history. Mosnier formally identifies the project as a joint Civil Rights History Project sponsored by the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress, and welcomes Purcell Maurice Conway.
related_events: Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

improvements: Could note Mosnier's slight delay in formally identifying the project (he apologizes for forgetting earlier on the tape).

chapters.title: Lincolnville Childhood and Great-Great-Grandmother
chapters.summary: Conway begins with his history of growing up in Lincolnville, which he describes as a 'heavenly place to grow up' where all the adults knew the children and watched out for them. After age five he was raised by his great-great-grandmother, Mrs. Robinson, whom he describes as of Seminole Indian and Spanish mixed heritage. She raised him to acknowledge adults, be respectful, not steal, and not lie. His mother, father, and three brothers moved to a new development in West Augustine on Scotts Street. Conway recalls running freely throughout Lincolnville from sunup to sundown without concern because the adult community watched over the children. He spent most of his time at the 'little links,' the same site that today houses the Galimore Center -- the only Black park in St. Augustine, where Mr. Eddie Lee Vickers ran the facility and offered ping pong, pool tables, tennis, archery, and other recreation without charge.

improvements: Mrs. Robinson's name is rendered in the transcript as 'Mr. Jorobinson' which is almost certainly a Whisper artifact; flagged here but represented as 'Mrs. Robinson' on the assumption of female caregiver context.

chapters.title: Paper Boy at the Castillo de San Marcos and Early Racial Lesson
chapters.summary: At age ten, Conway worked up the courage to cross Washington Street -- the boundary between Lincolnville and the white area -- to ask for a job at the St. Augustine Record. He got the position and was confined by his grandmother to selling papers within Lincolnville, which initially earned him only ten cents over multiple hours in the hot sun. Determined to make more money, he relocated to the Castillo de San Marcos in the downtown white area, where tourists bought every paper he carried -- on his first day he came home with several dollars, which he had never earned before. White paper boys then began following him, and on one day two of them positioned themselves to his left and right; customers coming out of the Castillo began buying from the white boys rather than from him. After several days of this, Conway moved to a less lucrative location and concluded the life of a paper boy was only a year or two long.

chapters.title: School, Work, and Witnessing His Mother's Treatment
chapters.summary: Conway describes working a series of menial jobs throughout St. Augustine -- washing dishes, washing pots, cleaning floors -- and becoming disenchanted with his high school's outdated textbooks (four or five years old), which led teachers to label him a 'troublemaking rabble rouser.' His uncle Edward Norton in New York had left a small library of books, including medical books, that he read avidly. He recounts becoming increasingly annoyed watching his mother Julia be addressed by white shopkeepers as 'Julia' while she was required to respond with 'Miss' and 'Mister.' He joined the DCT (Diversified Cooperative Training) program at his high school to receive credit for work, taking a form to the owner of a grocery store on Anastasia Island where he had been working with his father; the owner initially refused to sign, but relented when Conway threatened to quit, despite Conway's central role doing the store's accounting and bookkeeping for $40 a week.

improvements: Expanded the DCT acronym for clarity; transcript only uses 'DCT.'

chapters.title: Florida Memorial College and Introduction to Nonviolence
chapters.summary: Conway recounts that the only available swimming pool for Black children in St. Augustine was at Florida Memorial College, where he became aware of the Civil Rights Movement by listening to young male college students discuss demonstrations. They were trained in nonviolence as a tactic, which Conway adopted, though he says his ear at the time leaned more toward the more direct speakers, naming Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X. He had heard of Dr. (Martin Luther) King, finding him 'very informative and passionate,' but ultimately decided alongside the college students to embrace nonviolent direct action. He mentions a tragedy in which a couple of his high school friends drowned in a sinkhole behind R.J. Murray High School, underscoring the scarcity of safe recreation for Black children.

improvements: Chapter is short; could be merged with chapter 6 if a longer narrative arc is desired.

chapters.title: The Sandwich by the Dog Bowl: Mother's Awakening
chapters.summary: Conway tells the story of being asked by his mother to cut the lawn of one of her white friends on Anastasia Island. He charged a discounted ten dollars because of the friendship. When he finished in the heat, the woman invited him to take a cold drink and a sandwich; he found them placed next to her dog's tray, dog food on one side, his sandwich and water on the other. He asked to use her phone and called his mother Julia, who came immediately to pick him up. Conway recalls his mother telling the woman: 'You know something, miss so-and-so, I thought you was my friend. I cannot believe that you would treat my child this way.' This incident, Conway says, was the moment his mother began to understand what he had been describing about everyday racial mistreatment and started becoming involved in the movement herself.

chapters.title: Family Participation and Arrests in the Movement
chapters.summary: Conway participated in numerous marches after promising his mother he would not get arrested, since his great-great-grandmother depended on him at home. He took up positions near the rear of marches and recalls being chased by police but never caught. On one night his mother, father, and three brothers were all arrested, leaving him as the only family member out of jail. Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling, present for part of the interview, joins briefly. Conway reflects that being arrested for the movement became, at one point in St. Augustine, 'a badge of honor' among Black households, and that adults became totally committed when they saw their children and the teenagers demonstrating in the streets. Conway mentions classmate Samuel White (one grade below him) and James White, in his home-room class.
related_events: St. Augustine Movement / St. Augustine Campaign

chapters.title: First Wade-In at St. Augustine Beach and Klan Attack
chapters.summary: Conway describes participating in a demonstration at St. Augustine Beach to test the newly enacted Civil Rights Act. He went out with four other teens and a tall white man whose name he tentatively gives as 'Al Lengo,' wading into the surf. Within ten or fifteen minutes a large crowd of whites converged from north and south. As the group retreated to the car, a young Black girl in the group ran ahead, reached the car first, locked all the doors, and could not be persuaded to open them. The mob surrounded the group with bats, chains, and pipes pulled from a truck, threatening to bound them, weight them, and drop them out in the surf. A white kid punched Conway in the face, cutting his lip; Conway was prepared to fight back, but the white organizer placed his arm across Conway's chest and told him to stand his ground without fighting. The organizer was then struck in the head with a pipe and collapsed unconscious next to Conway, blood spattering Conway's face. A single police siren grew louder in the distance; the mob dispersed as a lone police car arrived. The organizer was taken to the hospital and the group was taken back to Washington Street, where Hosea Williams reacted with anger, vowing to come back stronger.
related_events: St. Augustine Movement / St. Augustine Campaign

errors: Conway gives the white organizer's name only tentatively ('I think his name was Al Lengo'); the summary preserves the name but the identification is uncertain.
improvements: Could explicitly flag Conway's hedge on the organizer's name.

chapters.title: Second Wade-In: Florida State Troopers Drive Out the Klan
chapters.summary: Two weeks after the first wade-in, a larger demonstration returned to St. Augustine Beach. Conway recalls disembarking the bus through a cordon of helmeted police and resolving to be at the front of the march. The state trooper in charge told the group that the troopers would have to first clear the water of hostile whites before the marchers could enter, promising they would 'go into the ocean today.' Florida state troopers then waded waist-deep into the surf, where Conway witnessed what he calls the most violent melee he has ever seen: the Klan and their sympathizers in the water carried two-by-fours, chains, and bats, and the troopers beat them into submission, dragging unconscious men ashore and returning for more. Conway describes being 'really confused' by the sight of white men beating white men for his civil rights. When the trooper signaled the water was safe, Conway found the surf so thick with blood that he did not enter. He attributes the troopers' arrival to Governor Farris Bryant; the interviewer Mosnier adds, drawing on Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling's comments, that wider forces -- including the US Justice Department -- were also engaged.

chapters.title: Police Dog Standoff and Tutorial Movie Theater Incident
chapters.summary: Conway describes accompanying a young white female tutor and a small class of Black children (the oldest about six or seven) at a tutorial school on Washington Street. Concerned that taking the children to a downtown movie theater was unwise so soon after the Civil Rights Act, he stayed near a church on the edge of Washington Street to wait. Within twenty minutes he heard a child scream and walked one block over toward the white area, where he saw the tutor running with one child by the hand pursued by a white male on a bicycle holding a knife. Conway intervened and was immediately surrounded by two policemen with a police dog. As friends from the Washington Street side approached, one pushed the policemen and ran, drawing both officers and the dog's handler off in pursuit; one officer told the German Shepherd 'if he moves, kill him.' Conway eventually turned slowly and sprinted back to Washington Street, with the dog not following. He hid in a church yard for two days and nights afterward, with community members bringing him water and sandwiches and assuring him his parents knew he was safe.

chapters.title: White Northern College Tutors Changed His Perspective
chapters.summary: Conway reflects that the white college students who came to St. Augustine to tutor Black children changed his perspective of whites; he had assumed all whites hated Black people. He names Gail Blattenberg, with whom he had recently reconnected by email, as a particular influence -- she acted 'like a mother' though only a few years older. The visiting tutors came from Princeton, New Jersey, Massachusetts, and other Northern states. When their session ended they invited a group of Black St. Augustine youth to spend two weeks at their homes in the North. Conway's mother initially refused; the white students responded by making picket signs and picketing in front of the Conway home until Julia Conway relented in laughter and allowed him to go. Conway stayed with Gail Blattenberg's family in Princeton, calling it 'a very good trip' that 'changed my whole perspective of white folk.'

improvements: 'Gail Blattenberg' spelling is Conway's recollection; the transcript renders it phonetically and the actual spelling may vary.

chapters.title: Trade School Discrimination and Move to New York
chapters.summary: Conway describes attending a junior college in Daytona Beach where he met his wife, then a student at Bethune-Cookman. He enrolled in a two-year brick masonry trade program with a Black instructor and a predominantly Black student body. About six months from the end of the program, two white students entered the course; within a few months they were allowed to take the certification test and become masons while the Black students who had completed a year and a half were not. Conway's instructor protested and was fired. The administration offered the Black students a 'settlement' requiring them to spend the final six months in a carpentry class to receive their two-year certificates; the students all refused. Conway received nothing for his year and a half. He married, moved to New York with his wife's parents, and worked five years in the post office, where again he found himself doing accounting work for a supervisor who could not do it but was paid more 'just to be over the blacks.' He notes that racism in the North was 'worse' than in the South -- 'a pat on the back and a smile on the face' and 'stab you, as often as I can' -- though the Civil Rights Act 'opened the floodgates' for Black opportunities in the post office.

chapters.title: NYPD Career and Discrimination Lawsuits Over Promotional Exams
chapters.summary: After five years in the post office, Conway passed the New York City Police Department's exam and was hired. He found the racism inside the department 'just as rampant' and 'more intimidating' due to its rank-and-file structure. Drawing on his St. Augustine movement training, he refused to accept disrespect from superiors regardless of their rank, demanding mutual respect as a human being. He notes that many Black officers who entered the department with him had come out of the Civil Rights Movement and were equally insistent on respect, with Black female officers being especially uncompromising. He joined a group lawsuit against the police department and the city of New York for discrimination on the promotional exam for sergeant; the suit was successful. He filed a similar suit for the lieutenant's exam, also successfully. The first lawsuit, on the sergeant's test, produced 'over 300 minority candidates,' whom Conway notes the department called 'quota sergeants.' Conway says the criteria established in those lawsuits were used around the nation and are still used today. He left as a lieutenant with honors, having trained hundreds of officers and never having had a serious complaint against any white male or female officer under his supervision.

chapters.title: Returning to St. Augustine: Disappointment, Red-Lining, and Civic Reflection
chapters.summary: Conway describes bringing his children back to St. Augustine every summer to visit his parents and brothers, with his mother enrolling them in a women's program at the YMCA. He says he is 'really disheartened at the non-improvement of black life' in St. Augustine, observing abandoned houses, drug-ravaged areas, and Black seniors unable to keep up their properties. He describes the pattern as red-lining of the kind he witnessed in New York: banks not lending, neighborhood key-removal, raising of taxes, and then gentrification, which he says has played out in Lincolnville over the previous ten years. He reflects on the 'buddy system' he encountered in the police department as a form of racism that his white colleagues did not recognize as such, since one's 'buddies' tended to be the people one lived around and drank with. He has moved back to Florida and now lives in Palm Coast. He returns to the Galimore Center (formerly the little links) as his current civic passion, noting the swimming pool has been closed for over two years and that he has petitioned the city commissioners with historical facts to reopen it.

chapters.title: Tribute to Parents and the ACCORD Organization
chapters.summary: At Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling's suggestion, Conway pays tribute to his parents Julia Conway and George Conway. He describes his father George as a delivery man for a grocery store on Anastasia Island who was well known and loved on both sides of the color line. When the store owner moved to fire George for participating in the marches, the store's white butcher told the owner 'if he goes, I go,' and the firing was reversed. Conway describes his mother Julia as 'extremely loving, extremely hard working,' who eventually got a job at Flagler Hospital training nurses how to handle newborns and giving them home remedies, but was fired before her tenth year so she could not collect a pension. Conway closes by praising the ACCORD organization led by Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and her family, and his neighbor Mrs. Barbara Vickers, who took on building a foot soldiers monument in St. Augustine. ACCORD operates on a volunteer basis as a 501(c)(3). The interview ends with Mosnier signing off on behalf of the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
related_events: ACCORD (Anniversary to Commemorate the Civil Rights Demonstrations)

errors: The ACCORD expansion ('Anniversary to Commemorate the Civil Rights Demonstrations') is the canonical St. Augustine ACCORD organization name, but is not spelled out in the transcript; the transcript only uses 'ACCORD.' The expansion is included for clarity but is an inference.
improvements: Consider dropping the ACCORD expansion if it cannot be confirmed within the transcript.

interview_metadata.interviewee_title: St. Augustine Civil Rights Activist; Retired New York City Police Lieutenant
interview_metadata.interview_location: Bayfront Hilton Hotel, St. Augustine, Florida
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Southern Oral History Program, University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill

evidence: Conway opens with his Lincolnville childhood and works chronologically through paper boy, school, movement participation, beach wade-ins, college discrimination, NYPD career, and return to St. Augustine (Segment 10, 00:01:43,000; Segment 364, 00:47:59,000; Segment 446, 00:55:01,000)
Closing tribute to parents and ACCORD provides reflective resolution (Segment 577, 01:07:25,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Conway delivers a clear chronological-thematic arc anchored by his St. Augustine youth and movement participation, with smooth transitions facilitated by Mosnier. The structure occasionally backtracks (the second beach wade-in interrupts the linear chronology to recount the consequence of the first) but the overall flow is logical and the interview reaches a satisfying reflective close on family and movement legacy.

most_vivid_moment.quote: But one white kid walked past me and he punched me in the face. I cut my lip over. ... And then he was hit. I recall over the head with a pipe. The blood hit me in the face. And he collapsed pretty much unconscious next to me.

evidence: Castillo de San Marcos paper-selling scene with white paper boys flanking him left and right (Segment 36-37, 00:08:47,000)
Sandwich placed by the dog's bowl with sandwich on one side and dog food on the other (Segment 84, 00:21:02,000)
Florida state troopers driving the Klan from the surf with the water 'so bloody' Conway would not enter (Segment 193-195, 00:35:27,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Conway provides exceptional specificity: named locations (Castillo de San Marcos, Anastasia Island, Washington Street, Scotts Street, Galimore Center, Flagler Hospital), named people (Eddie Lee Vickers, Al Lengo, Hosea Williams, Gail Blattenberg, Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan, Barbara Vickers, George Conway, Julia Conway), and reconstructed dialogue throughout ('You're dead. I told you not to go over there'; 'If he goes, I go'; 'If he moves, kill him'). The sensory texture of the bloody surf and the dog bowl tray is unusually visceral.

most_authentic_moment.quote: I recall standing there thinking, well, Lord, I didn't think I'd go out this way. ... I said, look, I have no white neighborhood. I would know it would run. There's no place to run.

vulnerability_elements: Admits readiness to abandon nonviolence under threat ('first guy that comes at me, I got to do what I got to do')
Names his confusion at white troopers beating white Klansmen on his behalf
Shares his fear of being beaten if arrested by the local police
Describes hiding in a churchyard for two days and nights
emotional_authenticity.justification: Conway is candid about fear, confusion, and the limits of nonviolent commitment under direct mob attack. He admits readiness to fight back at the first wade-in and his shock at watching state troopers beat the Klan into submission. The emotional palette is wide -- pride in his mother, anger at the dog-bowl insult, tenderness toward his great-great-grandmother, and amazement at white college tutors picketing his home -- and rarely feels performed.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Conway is a skilled narrative voice whose movement participation, family life, and police career interlock into a coherent arc. He moves between vivid scene-setting (the Castillo, the beach mob, the police dog) and reflective analysis (the 'buddy system' as racism, red-lining in Lincolnville). The interview rewards a listener with both dramatic eye-level testimony and earned wisdom from a long civic life.

major_events: First St. Augustine Beach wade-in attacked by white mob (Segment 124-160, 00:27:36,000-00:31:50,000)
Second St. Augustine Beach wade-in with Florida state troopers driving the Klan from the surf (Segment 165-200, 00:32:30,000-00:36:43,000)
Police dog standoff after intervening to protect a white tutor and Black children (Segment 230-271, 00:39:00,000-00:41:50,000)
NYPD sergeant promotional exam discrimination lawsuit producing over 300 minority sergeants (Segment 435-448, 00:54:12,000-00:55:22,000)

key_figures.name: Dr. Robert B. Hayling
key_figures.relationship: Movement leader Conway 'was like a fly in the wall' around; co-interview presence

key_figures.name: Dr. Martin Luther King Jr.
key_figures.relationship: Conway observed King during St. Augustine organizing

key_figures.name: Ralph Abernathy
key_figures.relationship: Conway observed Abernathy during St. Augustine organizing

key_figures.name: Hosea Williams
key_figures.relationship: Conway watched Williams react to the first wade-in attack and organize the second wade-in

key_figures.name: Julia Conway
key_figures.relationship: Mother; converted to active movement participation after sandwich-by-dog-bowl incident

key_figures.name: George Conway
key_figures.relationship: Father; movement participant whose white butcher coworker defended him from firing

key_figures.name: Gail Blattenberg
key_figures.relationship: Northern white college tutor whose Princeton family hosted Conway for two weeks

key_figures.name: Gwendolyn Duncan
key_figures.relationship: Founder/leader of ACCORD in St. Augustine

key_figures.name: Barbara Vickers
key_figures.relationship: Neighbor who led St. Augustine foot soldiers monument effort

organizational_involvement.role: Lieutenant; plaintiff in promotional-exam discrimination lawsuits

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: Conway is a youth-eyewitness foot soldier of the 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign at the ground level, including direct eyewitness testimony of both St. Augustine Beach wade-ins (the first mob attack and the second state-trooper intervention) and proximity to the leadership cluster of Hayling, King, Abernathy, and Hosea Williams.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Conway's vantage point as a teenage participant in St. Augustine -- the movement that triggered the political pressure for the Civil Rights Act of 1964 -- combined with his later career as a Black NYPD lieutenant who litigated promotional-exam discrimination gives this interview unusually wide historical reach. His direct eyewitness account of the second beach wade-in, in which state troopers beat Klansmen, is a specific and dramatic addition to the documentary record.

precision_examples: Conway's birth year: 'I was born in 1948' (Segment 61, 00:16:12,000)
Specific employer ($40 per week at the Anastasia Island grocery store, doing accounting and stocking; Segment 53, 00:13:41,000)
St. Augustine Beach wade-in: 'about two weeks later' a second demonstration was organized (Segment 165-166, 00:32:21,000)
Sergeant exam lawsuit: 'over 300 minority candidates' produced (Segment 447, 00:55:18,000)
Brick masonry program: 'year and a half' completed before two white students were given certificates after 'three months' (Segment 370-388, 00:48:34,000-00:50:01,000)
verifiable_claims: Florida state troopers ordered down to St. Augustine by Governor Farris Bryant to suppress Klan violence at the beach
NYPD sergeant promotional exam discrimination lawsuit produced over 300 minority sergeants
Hosea Williams organized the second St. Augustine Beach wade-in after the first was attacked
Conway's father George worked at a grocery store on Anastasia Island and was defended from firing by the store's white butcher
specificity_of_details.justification: Conway provides verifiable structural facts (the wade-ins, the troopers under Bryant, the NYPD lawsuits, the Daytona Beach trade school discrimination, Flagler Hospital employment of his mother) and named individuals (Hayling, Williams, Bryant, Blattenberg, Duncan, Vickers, Vickers father, Conway parents). Some specifics are hedged ('I think his name was Al Lengo,' 'two by fours, chains, and bats') but the overall density of verifiable detail is high.

new_information: Conway's youth-eyewitness foot-soldier vantage point on the St. Augustine wade-ins, including the moment a white organizer he tentatively identifies as 'Al Lengo' was struck unconscious with a pipe
His description of Florida state troopers physically beating Klansmen and their sympathizers in the surf as a peace-keeping intervention
The story of white Northern college tutors picketing his mother's home to convince her to let him visit them in Princeton, NJ for two weeks
The story of his father George's white butcher coworker threatening to quit if the grocery owner fired George for marching
Conway's role as a multi-suit plaintiff in NYPD promotional-exam discrimination litigation
understudied_aspects: Youth foot soldiers of the St. Augustine campaign (as distinct from the leadership cluster of Hayling, King, and Abernathy)
The afterlife of Southern civil-rights training in Northern institutional anti-discrimination lawsuits
Cross-racial labor solidarity at the St. Augustine local-business level (the white butcher's defense of George Conway)
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Conway complicates the conventional split between Northern liberalism and Southern racism by insisting that Northern racism was 'worse' in his post office and police experience, and by complicating the conventional split between activist Black community and passive white police by recounting state troopers beating Klansmen in the surf.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Historians of the St. Augustine campaign can use Conway's eyewitness wade-in testimony alongside Hayling's leadership account; labor historians can use the butcher's defense of George Conway as a working-class cross-racial solidarity moment; police-reform historians can use the NYPD sergeant lawsuit story as a movement-trained second-generation activism case.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Conway adds a teenage foot-soldier perspective to the St. Augustine campaign and extends the movement's institutional reach into Northern police reform litigation. Both contributions are substantial and not duplicative of the better-documented leadership voices in the campaign.

historical_value.overall_assessment: This interview's historical value is unusually high for a foot-soldier oral history. Conway combines direct eyewitness testimony of two canonical 1964 wade-in events at St. Augustine Beach with a long-arc institutional follow-on as a movement-trained NYPD lieutenant who used litigation to extend the movement's reach into the Northern public-sector workforce. The dual frame -- St. Augustine youth and NYPD officer -- makes the testimony useful to multiple historiographies.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Conway moves from a child confused by why white tourists at the Castillo would stop buying his papers when white boys flanked him, to a teenage demonstrator preparing to die in the surf, to a Northern police lieutenant who institutionalized anti-discrimination through lawsuits, and finally to an elder civic activist working to reopen the Galimore Center pool.
key_turning_points: First racial lesson at the Castillo de San Marcos (Segment 32-40, 00:07:38,000-00:10:23,000)
Mother's awakening after the dog-bowl sandwich incident (Segment 87-94, 00:21:27,000-00:22:25,000)
First St. Augustine Beach wade-in attack ('I didn't think I'd go out this way'; Segment 138, 00:29:12,000)
White state troopers beating Klansmen on his behalf ('I'm really confused now'; Segment 184-197, 00:34:29,000-00:35:49,000)
Two-week visit to Gail Blattenberg's family in Princeton, NJ (Segment 320-324, 00:44:59,000-00:45:10,000)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: Before: Conway believed 'most of the whites just hate black people' and had 'very little trust for pretty much whites.' After the second wade-in and the Northern trip with white tutors, his worldview opened.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Conway's transformation models the way civil-rights movement participation produces durable institutional courage: he carries the same insistence on respect into the NYPD and converts it into legal precedent.
transformation_growth.justification: Conway explicitly tracks his own evolution from suspicion to qualified trust of whites, and from teenage demonstrator to institutional litigator. The arc is reflective rather than hagiographic; he names the moments that changed him.

peak_moments.description: First St. Augustine Beach wade-in: mob attack with bats, chains, and pipes; white organizer struck unconscious next to Conway
peak_moments.stakes: Conway's life and the lives of four other teenagers

peak_moments.description: Second wade-in: Florida state troopers physically driving Klan from the surf
peak_moments.stakes: Whether the right to integrated beach access could be enforced

peak_moments.description: Police dog standoff after intervening to protect tutor and children
peak_moments.stakes: Conway's safety; potential mauling or arrest

peak_moments.description: Mother confronts white friend by phone after sandwich-by-dog-bowl incident
peak_moments.stakes: The Conway family's friendship with this white woman and the mother's political awakening

conflict_types: intra-family debate about how to respond to oppression
internal conflict over nonviolence under direct attack
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From terror (first wade-in) to bewilderment (second wade-in) to tender pride (mother's awakening) to vindication (NYPD lawsuit).
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Multiple high-stakes moments with vivid danger and clear consequences. The first wade-in is among the most dramatic foot-soldier wade-in accounts in the CRHP corpus, and the police-dog standoff has clear life-or-death stakes.

universal_themes: child finding his way in a hostile world
friendship and the betrayal of friendship across racial lines
the slow institutional work of dignity
relatable_struggles: wanting to make money as a child to buy candy
being underestimated and undervalued at work
human_moments: great-great-grandmother lining up his shirt for his first day as a paper boy (Segment 28, 00:06:24,000)
white Northern tutors picketing his mother's home with cardboard signs to convince her to let him travel (Segment 313-320, 00:44:38,000-00:44:59,000)
white butcher telling the grocery owner 'if he goes, I go' to defend George Conway (Segment 607-615, 01:10:08,000-01:10:44,000)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Family love, the indignity of being underestimated, and the small acts of cross-racial solidarity at the workplace level are accessible to audiences with no direct civil-rights movement background.
relatable_experiences.justification: The human-scale moments (the great-great-grandmother, the dog-bowl sandwich, the butcher's defense of George Conway) translate Conway's civil-rights testimony into universally legible terms about family, work, and friendship.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Conway's interview balances ground-level dramatic moments with reflective synthesis. The wade-in danger, the police-dog standoff, and the family arrests carry strong emotional intensity; the parental tribute and the ACCORD recognition close with warmth and gratitude rather than triumphalism.

barriers: Conway's syntax occasionally fragments in the transcript ('I would say, wow, I guess that treat me great'), partly an artifact of Whisper transcription and partly conversational rhythm
Acronyms like DCT and ACCORD are used without expansion
Some references to local St. Augustine landmarks ('the little links,' 'Washington Street') assume local knowledge
language_clarity.justification: Conway's speech is generally clear and self-contextualizing, with occasional unspecified acronyms (DCT, ACCORD) and locally specific landmark names that an outside listener would need to map. Whisper-induced syntax breaks ('Mr. Jorobinson,' 'in this group in a week') slightly impede comprehension in places.

high_energy_sections: First wade-in mob attack (Segment 124-162, 00:27:36,000-00:32:01,000) -- sustained tension and physical danger
Second wade-in trooper intervention (Segment 165-200, 00:32:30,000-00:36:43,000) -- ongoing peak
Police dog standoff (Segment 232-263, 00:39:22,000-00:41:19,000) -- escalating threat resolved by sprint
slow_sections: Mid-interview discussion of NYPD buddy-system racism (Segment 485-510, 00:59:23,000-01:02:13,000) -- analytical rather than narrative
Closing tribute discussion of pension and Flagler Hospital (Segment 622-650, 01:11:24,000-01:13:12,000) -- looser pacing
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: An attentive listener can follow the full 77-minute arc; the wade-in cluster and the police-dog standoff carry the middle, and the parental tribute carries the end.
narrative_flow.justification: Mosnier facilitates without interrupting and prompts effectively for chronological transitions. The pace varies appropriately between dramatic narrative (wade-ins) and analytical reflection (buddy system, red-lining).

themes_connected: the relationship between Southern civil rights movement experience and Northern institutional anti-discrimination work
the cross-generational continuity of movement training
the political economy of gentrification, red-lining, and neighborhood decline
the slow institutional gains produced by movement-era lawsuits
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Conway's red-lining and gentrification analysis of Lincolnville, and his observation that NYPD anti-discrimination criteria are 'still being used at this day,' tie his testimony to present-day urban-policy and police-reform conversations.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Conway repeatedly draws explicit connections between his St. Augustine teenage demonstration experience and his later refusal to accept disrespect from superiors in the NYPD, helping audiences see why movement training had durable institutional effects.
broader_themes_connection.justification: Conway is reflective and explicit about how his movement experience shaped his later career and how his urban-policy observations connect to broader patterns of housing discrimination. The analytical thread is consistent without being heavy-handed.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Accessible to a general audience with occasional St. Augustine-specific landmark and acronym friction. The interview rewards a listener with patience to follow Conway's chronological arc; Mosnier's gentle prompts ensure the narrative doesn't stall.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a high-value oral history that combines first-person foot-soldier testimony of the 1963-64 St. Augustine campaign -- including dramatic accounts of two beach wade-ins and a police-dog confrontation -- with a long-arc institutional follow-on as a Black NYPD lieutenant who litigated promotional-exam discrimination. Conway is articulate, candid about fear and confusion, and reflective about how his movement experience shaped his later career. The interview's strongest moments are the wade-in cluster, the sandwich-by-dog-bowl incident that converted his mother into an activist, and the closing tribute to the white butcher who defended his father George from being fired for marching. The narrative occasionally suffers from Whisper-induced transcript artifacts but the underlying testimony is coherent, specific, and historically valuable.
key_strengths: First-person teenage eyewitness testimony of both 1964 St. Augustine Beach wade-ins, including the rare account of Florida state troopers physically driving Klansmen from the surf

Long arc connecting Southern civil-rights movement training to Northern institutional anti-discrimination litigation, including the NYPD sergeant promotional-exam lawsuit that produced over 300 minority sergeants

Rich human-scale moments (sandwich-by-dog-bowl, white butcher defending George Conway, white tutors picketing his mother's home) that translate the civil-rights story into universally accessible terms
key_limitations: Some named identifications are tentative or hedged ('I think his name was Al Lengo'); a careful reader must preserve the hedge

Whisper transcription introduces syntactic artifacts and ambiguous renderings (e.g., 'Mr. Jorobinson' for Conway's great-great-grandmother, fragmented sentences in the NYPD discussion) that can obscure meaning

claims.claim: Conway was interviewed in St. Augustine, Florida on September 13, 2011 by Joseph Mosnier of the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill, with videographer John Bishop, for the Civil Rights History Project jointly sponsored by the Library of Congress and the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Today is Tuesday, September 13, 2011. My name is Joe Mosnier, I'm the Southern Oral History Program at the University of North Carolina at Chapel Hill. ... I'm with the videographer John Bishop, we are in St. Augustine Florida at the Bayfront Hilton Hotel. ... This is a civil rights history project interview for joint sponsors, the Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture and the Library of Congress (Segments 2-9, 00:00:10,000-00:01:43,000)
claims.rationale: All elements directly stated in the transcript opening.

claims.claim: Conway grew up in Lincolnville, the historically Black neighborhood of St. Augustine, raised after age five by his great-great-grandmother, Mrs. Robinson, who was of Seminole Indian and Spanish descent.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I started by just my history of growing up in St. Augustine, particularly Lincolnville, it's a black, self-sufficient community. ... I was raised after the age of five by my great-great-grandmother, Mr. Jorobinson. She was a Seminole Indian and Spanish mixed. (Segments 10, 12, 00:01:43,000-00:02:17,000)
claims.rationale: All elements supported. The transcript renders the surname phonetically as 'Mr. Jorobinson,' a likely Whisper artifact; the summary normalizes to 'Mrs. Robinson' (female great-great-grandmother).

claims.claim: The Galimore Center, then the only Black recreational park in the city, was a sanctuary in an otherwise segregated environment.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I spent most of my time, particularly at the little links, which is also housing the Galimore Center today. It was the only black park in St. Augustine. It's the only place blacks could go for recreation. (Segments 16-17, 00:03:29,000-00:04:01,000) ... That was the only sanctuary you could go to to get away from all this hatred and bigotry and discrimination. (Segment 537, 01:04:03,000)

claims.claim: At age ten he sold newspapers for the St. Augustine Record, including in front of the Castillo de San Marcos, where his early success was disrupted when white paper boys were stationed beside him and customers redirected their purchases.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I worked up the courage one day. I was 10 years old to venture over there and ask for a job. ... I came over here one day, at the Castillo de San Marcos. I stood in front of the Castillo de San Marcos. Within about an hour, I sold every paper I had. ... I had two white paper boys to follow me up to the Castillo de San Marcos. They took positions to my left and right. ... they would come out of the Castillo de San Marcos. They'd take a look and they'd buy from me either one of the white kids. (Segments 24-38, 00:05:25,000-00:09:40,000)

claims.claim: Conway became aware of civil rights activism in his teens, around age fourteen or fifteen, after listening to Florida Memorial College students discuss demonstrations and nonviolent direct action.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So at least the college memorial opened up their pool in the summer to a lot of the black kids. Now that's when I got involved with listening to some of the young male college students talking about demonstrating, talking about civil rights and talking about doing something about it. (Segment 60, 00:15:48,000) ... I actually started getting involved when I was about 14 or 15. (Segment 61, 00:16:12,000)
claims.rationale: Supported. The 'Florida Memorial College' name is consistent with the transcript reference to 'Florida Memorial College' earlier in segment 58.

claims.claim: While he respected Dr. (Martin Luther) King, Conway was drawn at the time more toward the rhetoric of Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And my ear at the time was leaning more toward some of the more, I wouldn't say radical. But the more direct speakers like Stokely Carmichael and Malcolm X. I had heard of this, Dr. (Martin Luther) King. I found him to be very informative and passionate. (Segments 62-63, 00:16:40,000-00:17:09,000)
claims.rationale: Directly supported, with Conway's own qualifier preserved.

claims.claim: Conway joined the nonviolent demonstrations alongside other St. Augustine youth.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So that's what we decided. And I decided with them to embrace. Now when the demonstration started here, yes, I remember the kids going up to sit at the counters and what have you. So I told my mother, I'm going to start demonstrating. (Segments 63-64, 00:17:30,000-00:17:47,000)

claims.claim: A pivotal incident occurred in which a white family friend served Conway a sandwich next to her dog's bowl while he cut her lawn, prompting his mother Julia Conway to become an activist herself.
claims.supporting_excerpt: So when I walked up, she had sit it down next to her dog, dog's tray, dog food on this side, my sandwich and water on the other side. (Segment 84, 00:21:02,000) ... And that's when she started getting involved. (Segment 94, 00:22:18,000) ... My mother, Julia Conway (Segment 577, 01:07:25,000)
claims.rationale: Directly supported; Julia Conway's full name is given in the closing tribute and the lawn-mowing exchange is recounted in segments 76-93.

claims.claim: Conway's mother, father, and three brothers were arrested on one occasion.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Because one particular night my mother, my father, and my three brothers were all arrested. So I was only one out of jail. (Segments 102-103, 00:23:38,000-00:23:44,000)

claims.claim: A wade-in demonstration at St. Augustine Beach was organized in the wake of the Civil Rights Act, in which a white organizer he identifies as 'Al Lengo' was beaten unconscious by a mob carrying bats, pipes, and chains.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now I particularly now recall after this, so right now it was passed around where we were kind of pressed to get people to go out to test the newly enacted ... newly enacted law. (Segments 122-123, 00:26:49,000-00:27:17,000) ... He told me so tall white man. I think his name was Al Lengo. (Segment 125, 00:27:36,000) ... bats and chains. (Segment 150, 00:30:36,000) ... they pulled out angular weights. (Segment 136, 00:31:06,000)... I recall over the head with a pipe. ... And he collapsed pretty much unconscious next to me. (Segments 153-154, 00:30:59,000-00:31:04,000)
claims.rationale: Conway hedges on the organizer's name ('I think his name was Al Lengo'); the summary preserves the name without the hedge. The 'in the wake of the Civil Rights Act' framing is loose -- Conway references 'the newly enacted law' but does not pin the wade-in chronologically to pre- or post-passage. The mob attack with bats, pipes, and chains is supported.

claims.claim: A subsequent wade-in saw Florida state troopers, whom Conway believed had been ordered down by Governor Farris Bryant, drive Klansmen and their sympathizers out of the surf in a violent melee.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Florida state troopers, to force this angry mob out of the water. ... The wipes in the water had two by fours, chains, bats, and the fight was on. ... the Klan in their sympathizers were beaten into submission by white troopers. (Segments 179-183, 00:33:50,000-00:34:29,000) ... I guess it was. I think they were ordered down here from Farris Bryant. Governor just decided we're just going to not keep having this happen. (Segments 203-204, 00:36:32,000-00:36:40,000)
claims.rationale: Directly supported, with Conway's hedge ('I think they were ordered down here from Farris Bryant') preserved in the summary phrasing 'whom Conway believed had been ordered down.'

claims.claim: Conway describes being cornered by two policemen and a German Shepherd in the white area near the St. Augustine Record after intervening to protect a young white tutor and her Black students.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was surrounded by two hulking policemen. ... But they had a police dog. (Segments 233-236, 00:39:22,000-00:39:37,000) ... The German Shepherd and the policeman could not catch my two friends. (Segment 248, 00:40:15,000) ... over to the one block over where the St. Augustine record is located (Segment 227, 00:38:50,000)

claims.claim: Conway recalls Hosea Williams organizing the second wade-in.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I never get Hosea Williams was so angry. He said, oh, they think they're going to scare us away from the beach. We're going to come back stronger. So with that, a second demonstration was organized. (Segments 163-165, 00:32:01,000-00:32:21,000)

claims.claim: Conway was a 'fly on the wall' as Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling, Dr. (Martin Luther) King, (Ralph) Abernathy, and Hosea Williams convened in St. Augustine.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I was just amazed with Dr. (Robert B.) Hayling. When I finally got to know who he was and Dr. (Martin Luther) King (Ralph) Abernathy, Hosea Williams and all these people started coming around. I was like a flower in a wall. (Segments 119-120, 00:26:18,000-00:26:38,000)
claims.rationale: Directly supported. The transcript renders 'fly on the wall' as 'flower in a wall' (Whisper artifact); summary corrects to the idiomatic form.

claims.claim: White college student tutors from Princeton, Massachusetts, and other Northern locations -- including Gail Blattenberg, with whom he later reconnected -- changed his view of white people; he stayed two weeks with her family in Princeton, New Jersey after his mother relented to a picketing campaign the students staged in front of his home.
claims.supporting_excerpt: You had one particular college student, Gail Blattenberg. I recently contacted her about two months ago. And through emails. (Segments 289-291, 00:43:10,000-00:43:27,000) ... She was from Princeton, New Jersey at the time. You had some other kids from Massachusetts (Segments 296-297, 00:43:48,000-00:43:51,000) ... they made up picket signs and picket. They were like, hey, I'm going to go to the house to try to get my mother to relent and let me go for that two-week trip with them. (Segments 313-315, 00:44:38,000-00:44:43,000) ... I stayed with the Gil Blatt and Berg and her parents. (Segment 321, 00:45:03,000)
claims.rationale: Directly supported; the transcript renders Blattenberg's name phonetically and the summary preserves the recollection.

claims.claim: After enrolling in a brick masonry program at a Daytona Beach junior college and watching two white students receive their certificates after only a few months while he and his Black classmates were denied theirs, Conway moved to New York with his wife (whom he met at Bethune-Cookman).
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now, I want to go into a junior college in Daytona Beach where I met my wife who was going to Bethune-Cookman. (Segment 364, 00:47:59,000) ... Now, I took up a brick masonry. (Segment 366, 00:48:09,000) ... within a few months, they were allowed to go take the test and become a mason. (Segment 373, 00:48:48,000) ... But fortunately, I married and my wife was from New York. We moved back with herf parents in New York (Segments 394-395, 00:50:18,000-00:50:27,000)

claims.claim: He worked five years in the Post Office, then joined the New York City Police Department, where he led successful discrimination lawsuits over the sergeant's and lieutenant's promotional exams that produced over 300 minority sergeants and became national legal precedents.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I worked in post office my first five years. (Segment 400, 00:51:01,000) ... after five years I passed police department's test and went into that. (Segment 410, 00:51:53,000) ... I got together with a group, the Suits, the police department, and the city of New York for discriminating on the promotional exams. And we were successful. I again had the suit with the same similar suit for my lieutenants exam, and we were successful. (Segments 435-438, 00:54:02,000-00:54:28,000) ... It produced over 300 minority candidates. (Segment 447, 00:55:18,000) ... And those lawsuits were used, the criteria was used around the nation. And they were still being used at this day. (Segments 439-440, 00:54:28,000-00:54:37,000)

claims.claim: Conway closes by paying tribute to his mother Julia Conway, his father George Conway (whose white butcher coworker threatened to quit if his employer fired George for marching), and the ACCORD organization led by Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and Mrs. Barbara Vickers, which has worked to memorialize the St. Augustine foot soldiers.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yeah, my dear, loving Mother Julia Conway and my dedicated father, George Conway. (Segment 577, 01:07:25,000) ... the butcher walked up to the answer, I tell you what? If he goes, I go. (Segment 607, 01:10:08,000) ... they Mrs. Gwendolyn Duncan and her family took this task on by themselves some years ago (Segment 681, 01:15:16,000) ... my neighbor again, Mrs. Barbara Vickers. I recall when she took on her loan test of trying to make a monument for the foot soldiers. (Segments 685-686, 01:15:51,000-01:16:05,000)

publication_decision.rationale: Main summary accuracy score is 84 (below the 90 threshold) due to two acknowledged soft spots: (1) the wade-in organizer's name 'Al Lengo' is preserved without Conway's hedge, and (2) the 'in the wake of the Civil Rights Act' chronological framing is impressionistic relative to what the transcript pins down. The citation audit shows one partially_supported claim (the same wade-in framing). Quality score of 88 is also under the 90 threshold for comprehensiveness/depth gates. Routing to human review is the correct fail-closed outcome: a reviewer should decide whether to soften the wade-in framing and re-issue, or to publish with the hedges restored.
